course_info.title: Programming Fundamentals II
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Understand the principles of object-oriented programming
Define classes with attributes and methods in Python
readings: Think Python, Ch. 15-16 - Classes and Objects

lecture_notes.introduction: Programming Fundamentals II shifts focus from procedural programming to object-oriented design. OOP lets you model real-world entities as objects that bundle data and behavior, making complex programs more organized and maintainable.
key_concepts: Classes as blueprints: attributes store state, methods define behavior
The __init__ method and self parameter
Instance vs class attributes
Encapsulation: bundling data with the methods that operate on it
Object identity, equality, and representation (__str__, __repr__)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a BankAccount class with deposit, withdraw, and balance methods, demonstrating encapsulation and state management
setup_requirements: Familiarity with functions and data structures from CS101

review.value: A class is a template; an instance is a specific object created from that template. Each instance has its own attribute values but shares method definitions with all instances of the class
Every instance method receives self as its first parameter, which refers to the specific instance the method was called on, allowing access to that instance's attributes

learning_objectives: Override methods in subclasses to specialize behavior
Apply polymorphism to write flexible, extensible code

lecture_notes.introduction: Inheritance lets you build new classes from existing ones, reusing code and establishing is-a relationships. Polymorphism lets different classes respond to the same method call in different ways, enabling flexible designs.
key_concepts: Inheritance syntax and the is-a relationship
Method overriding and the super() function
Polymorphism: different types, same interface
isinstance() and type checking
Multiple inheritance basics and method resolution order
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a Shape hierarchy with Circle, Rectangle, and Triangle subclasses, each implementing area() and perimeter() methods differently
setup_requirements: Understanding of classes from previous session

review.value: Python uses C3 linearization to determine the order in which base classes are searched when looking up a method, preventing the diamond problem
A subclass instance should be usable wherever a parent class instance is expected without breaking the program; this is the foundation of polymorphism

learning_objectives: Use try/except blocks to handle runtime errors gracefully
Raise custom exceptions for domain-specific error conditions
Design programs that fail informatively rather than silently
readings: Think Python, Ch. 14 - Files (exception handling section)

lecture_notes.introduction: Robust programs anticipate failure. Exception handling lets you separate normal logic from error-handling logic, producing programs that recover gracefully or fail with clear diagnostics instead of crashing with cryptic tracebacks.
key_concepts: try / except / else / finally blocks
Exception hierarchy: BaseException, Exception, and built-in types
Raising exceptions with raise
Custom exception classes
EAFP vs LBYL: Easier to Ask Forgiveness vs Look Before You Leap
lecture_notes.demonstration: Refactor a file-processing script to handle FileNotFoundError, ValueError on parsing, and PermissionError, with informative messages for each
setup_requirements: Sample data files with intentional errors provided on LMS

review.value: Pythonic style prefers trying an operation and handling the exception (try/except) over checking conditions first (if/else); this is often faster and more readable
except: with no exception type catches everything including SystemExit and KeyboardInterrupt; always specify the exception type you expect

learning_objectives: Understand recursive function design with base and recursive cases
Trace recursive execution using the call stack model
Apply recursion to problems like tree traversal and divide-and-conquer

lecture_notes.introduction: Recursion is a function calling itself with a smaller input until reaching a base case. It is the natural way to process recursive data structures like trees and nested directories, and it underpins algorithms like merge sort and quicksort.
key_concepts: Base case and recursive case
The call stack and stack frames
Recursive vs iterative solutions: tradeoffs
Recursion on nested structures: directories, JSON, trees
Stack overflow and recursion limits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement factorial, Fibonacci, and a recursive directory listing function, visualizing the call stack at each step

review.value: Every recursive function must have at least one base case that returns a result without making a recursive call; without it, the function recurses infinitely and crashes with a stack overflow
Break a problem into smaller subproblems of the same type, solve each recursively, and combine the results; this strategy yields efficient algorithms like merge sort (O(n log n))

learning_objectives: Organize code into modules and packages
Apply test-driven development to build reliable software

lecture_notes.introduction: Professional software is organized into modules and validated by tests. This session covers how to structure projects beyond single files and how to write tests that catch bugs before users do.
key_concepts: import, from...import, and the module search path
Creating packages with __init__.py
The pytest framework: test functions, assertions, fixtures
Test-driven development (TDD): red, green, refactor
Code coverage and what it does (and does not) guarantee
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a calculator module with add, subtract, multiply, divide functions, then write a complete pytest test suite before implementing the divide-by-zero handling
setup_requirements: Project structured with src/ and tests/ directories

review.value: Write a failing test first (red), write the minimum code to pass it (green), then improve the code without changing behavior (refactor); this cycle produces well-tested, well-designed code
When you import a module, Python searches: the current directory, PYTHONPATH entries, and the standard library installation directory, in that order

learning_objectives: Analyze algorithm efficiency using Big-O notation
Implement and compare linear search and binary search
Understand basic sorting algorithms and their time complexity
readings: Grokking Algorithms, Ch. 1 - Binary Search and Big O

lecture_notes.introduction: Not all solutions are equal. Two programs can produce the same output but differ by orders of magnitude in speed. Big-O notation gives us a language to compare algorithms and choose the right tool for the problem size.
key_concepts: Big-O notation: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2)
Linear search: O(n) and when it is appropriate
Binary search: O(log n) and the sorted-input requirement
Selection sort and insertion sort: O(n^2)
Space complexity and the time-space tradeoff
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement linear and binary search, then time them on lists of 100, 10,000, and 1,000,000 elements to observe the performance difference empirically

review.value: Describes how an algorithm's running time grows as the input size grows; O(n) means doubling the input roughly doubles the time, O(n^2) means doubling the input quadruples the time
Binary search requires the input to be sorted; it achieves O(log n) by halving the search space on each step, checking whether the target is in the left or right half

questions.question: Define a class called Rectangle with width and height attributes. Include an area() method that returns the area and a __str__ method that returns 'Rectangle(width x height)'.

rubric.key: Correct area() method returning width * height

questions.question: What is the difference between a try/except/else block and a try/except/finally block? When would you use each?
questions.answer: The else block runs only if no exception occurred in the try block, and is used for code that should only execute on success. The finally block runs regardless of whether an exception occurred, and is used for cleanup code (closing files, releasing resources) that must execute no matter what.

rubric.key: Correctly explains else runs only on success
Provides appropriate use case for each

questions.question: Write a recursive function flatten(nested_list) that takes a list which may contain other lists and returns a single flat list of all elements. For example, flatten([1, [2, 3], [4, [5, 6]]]) returns [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6].

rubric.key: Correct base case (non-list items appended directly)
Correct recursive case (lists are flattened recursively)
Uses isinstance check for list type

questions.question: What are the three steps of test-driven development (TDD), and why is the order important?
questions.answer: 1. Red: Write a failing test that defines the desired behavior. 2. Green: Write the minimum code to make the test pass. 3. Refactor: Improve the code without changing its behavior while keeping tests green. The order matters because writing the test first forces you to think about the interface before implementation, writing minimum code prevents over-engineering, and refactoring with passing tests gives confidence that improvements don't break anything.

rubric.key: Explains the purpose of each step

major_assignments.title: Object-Oriented Library System
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a library management system using object-oriented programming. The system should model books, patrons, and loans with proper class hierarchies, support checkout/return operations, and include comprehensive error handling and a pytest test suite.
objectives: Design a class hierarchy modeling real-world entities and relationships
Apply encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism in a practical system
Implement robust error handling for invalid operations
Write a comprehensive test suite validating all major functionality
deliverables: Python package with library system implementation (multiple modules)
pytest test suite with at least 20 test cases covering all major operations
UML class diagram showing class relationships
Design document (2-3 pages) explaining architecture decisions

criteria: Class hierarchy properly models the domain (Book, Patron, Loan, Library)
Inheritance is used meaningfully (e.g., different book types)
Encapsulation prevents invalid state transitions
Polymorphism enables flexible handling of different item types
Checkout and return operations work correctly with date tracking
Search functionality by title, author, and ISBN
Overdue detection and fine calculation
Patron history and current loans tracking
At least 20 test cases covering normal and edge cases
Tests cover error conditions (checkout unavailable book, return wrong book)
Tests are well-organized and clearly named
All tests pass
UML diagram accurately reflects the implementation
Design document explains key architecture decisions
Code has docstrings for all classes and public methods
README explains how to run the system and tests
Well-designed class hierarchy demonstrating OOP mastery
Complete library operations with robust error handling
Comprehensive test suite validating all major functionality
Professional documentation including UML diagram and design rationale

major_assignments.title: Data Processing Pipeline
major_assignments.description: Build a modular data processing pipeline that reads CSV data, transforms it through a chain of configurable processing steps, and outputs results in multiple formats. Apply algorithm design principles and analyze the time complexity of your solution.
objectives: Design a modular, extensible processing pipeline using OOP
Implement efficient algorithms for sorting, filtering, and aggregation
Analyze and document the time complexity of each pipeline stage
Handle malformed data gracefully with comprehensive error reporting
deliverables: pytest test suite with at least 15 test cases
Complexity analysis document (1-2 pages) with Big-O for each stage
Sample output demonstrating all processing features

criteria: Pipeline stages are modular and independently testable
New processing steps can be added without modifying existing code
Configuration is data-driven, not hardcoded
Clear separation between I/O and processing logic
Sorting, filtering, and aggregation produce correct results
Algorithms are implemented efficiently for the data size
Edge cases are handled (empty data, duplicate keys, missing values)
Complexity analysis is accurate and well-explained
Malformed CSV rows are logged and skipped, not crashed on
Type conversion errors produce clear diagnostics
Missing required columns are detected early with helpful messages
Pipeline continues processing after recoverable errors
Tests cover each pipeline stage independently
Tests verify error handling with malformed inputs
Tests validate end-to-end pipeline behavior
All tests pass and are clearly named
Modular pipeline architecture enabling easy extension
Efficient algorithms with accurate complexity analysis
Robust error handling that never crashes on bad data
Comprehensive test coverage for all stages and error paths

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of programming concepts and OOP principles

lab_assignments.description: Weekly hands-on programming exercises building OOP and testing skills

major_assignments.description: Larger projects integrating OOP, testing, and algorithm design

midterm_examination.description: Written and coding exam covering OOP and exception handling

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all course material

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts is encouraged, but all code must be written independently. Pair programming is allowed on labs only when explicitly assigned.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted code must be your own work. Use of AI code generators must be disclosed. Submitting code written by another person or AI without attribution constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Python 3.12+, VS Code, and pytest installed. Git familiarity is expected. Lab computers are available but personal machines are recommended.
